channel.title: Digital Assets Morning Wire
channel.description: Daily digital-asset regulatory and market digest. AI-sourced and AI-generated — verify against official sources before acting.

item.title: Website alert: trustwallet[.]uk[.]com
item.description: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority warned the public that the website trustwallet[.]uk[.]com refers to a 'Trust Wallet' service that holds no licence under Hong Kong's Payment Systems and Stored Value Facilities Ordinance to issue, or facilitate the issue of, stored-value facilities in Hong Kong. So what: The flagged domain can be added to fraud and phishing watchlists, and the alert is a reminder that clients in Hong Kong may be targeted by unlicensed wallet services impersonating well-known crypto brands. [official]

item.title: JPMorgan says bitcoin outlook sees ‘encouraging sign’ as Strategy boosts cash reserves
item.description: JPMorgan analysts said strengthening activity in bitcoin futures points to renewed institutional interest, while the corporate bitcoin holder Strategy lifted its cash reserves to 3 billion United States dollars so it can pay preferred dividends without being forced to sell bitcoin. So what: This is market commentary rather than regulatory news; its relevance is limited to background awareness of institutional bitcoin sentiment when reviewing client and counterparty digital-asset exposure. [single source]

item.title: ‘We’re going to get it done’: Crypto bill faces what could be final test over Trump conflicts of interest
item.description: United States Senate negotiators are still working through conflict-of-interest provisions covering officials including President Trump that stand between the Clarity Act — a bill setting out which federal agencies would regulate digital-asset markets — and a Senate floor vote targeted before the August recess. So what: If enacted, the Clarity Act would settle which United States regulators oversee digital-asset trading and custody, a structural change that would reshape the rulebooks United States exchange and custody counterparties operate under. [single source]

item.title: $1.9 trillion asset manager T. Rowe Price bets on active management with first multi-token crypto ETF
item.description: United States asset manager T. Rowe Price launched the industry's first actively managed exchange-traded fund holding a basket of spot cryptoassets, trading under the ticker TKNZ, with portfolio managers adjusting allocations across bitcoin, ether and several other tokens. So what: Actively managed multi-token funds from mainstream asset managers extend regulated, exchange-listed crypto exposure to conventional portfolios, widening the range of clients whose digital-asset holdings sit inside familiar securities-account channels. [single source]

item.title: Visa backs Open USD with new stablecoin platform as Circle faces fresh competition
item.description: Visa launched a stablecoin platform that lets banks and other financial institutions issue, store, transfer and redeem stablecoins across its payment network, starting with Open USD, a new stablecoin from a consortium whose backers include Visa, BlackRock, Alphabet and Coinbase. So what: Card-network infrastructure that puts stablecoin issuance and redemption inside regulated banks brings corresponding anti-money-laundering, sanctions-screening and reserve-oversight duties, making this a concrete reference point for how bank stablecoin services are likely to be structured. [corroborated]

item.title: $1.9 trillion asset manager T. Rowe Price launches first actively managed multi-token crypto ETF
item.description: T. Rowe Price began trading the T. Rowe Price Crypto ETF, described as the first actively managed multi-token spot crypto exchange-traded fund, on the New York Stock Exchange's Arca market with about 15 million United States dollars in assets weighted mainly to bitcoin and ether. So what: A mainstream asset manager running an actively managed basket of cryptoassets in an exchange-traded wrapper broadens the routes through which bank clients can hold digital-asset exposure inside ordinary securities accounts. [single source]

item.title: Visa launching internal stablecoin platform for clients that provides access to OUSD: report
item.description: Fortune reported that Visa is building a platform for its bank and merchant clients to mint, redeem and use Open USD (OUSD), a stablecoin from the Open Standard consortium of more than 140 companies, alongside Visa's existing support for Circle's USD Coin and Paxos's Global Dollar. So what: Free, unlimited minting and redemption of a consortium stablecoin through a card network would concentrate significant payment flows in one instrument, raising questions about reserve quality, redemption controls and screening coverage that compliance functions will want answered before such rails are used. [corroborated]

item.title: US jury indicts South Dakota crypto investor on 29 counts over alleged $20 million investor fraud
item.description: A United States federal grand jury indicted South Dakota investor Benjamin Wiener on 29 counts including wire fraud, money laundering, bank fraud and aggravated identity theft over an alleged 20 million dollar Ponzi-style scheme that took cash and digital currency from dozens of investors and moved it through banks and crypto exchanges to hide its origin. So what: The alleged laundering pattern — investor funds layered through bank accounts and crypto exchanges before personal spending — is a useful typology for transaction-monitoring scenarios covering accounts linked to informal investment schemes. [single source]

item.title: Crypto brokerage firm Alpaca raises $135 million for tokenized stock infrastructure
item.description: Crypto brokerage Alpaca raised 135 million United States dollars in equity, with debt financing lifting the total package to 435 million, to expand the infrastructure behind tokenised United States equities, a market in which it says it clears or custodies about 94 percent of products. So what: The concentration of most tokenised United States equity clearing and custody in a single infrastructure provider is a counterparty-concentration point worth noting when assessing exposure to tokenised-security products. [single source]

item.title: A crypto risk management framework for financial institutions
item.description: Blockchain analytics firm Elliptic published a risk-management framework arguing that financial institutions can cover cryptoasset exposure within their existing compliance programmes by mapping it onto customer, geographic, product and on-chain behavioural risk categories aligned with Financial Action Task Force (FATF) and Wolfsberg Group standards. So what: The framework is a usable benchmark for the live design question of whether digital-asset risk should sit inside the existing anti-money-laundering risk assessment or in a stand-alone programme. [single source]

item.title: Tether invests $20 million in Ualá as the fintech firm reaches $3.2 billion valuation
item.description: Stablecoin issuer Tether invested 20 million United States dollars in Ualá, an Argentine digital bank with more than 11 million customers and banking licences in Argentina, Mexico and Colombia, as part of a funding round valuing the firm at 3.2 billion dollars. So what: Stablecoin issuers taking stakes in licensed retail banking platforms in Latin America point to deeper links between regulated deposit-taking and stablecoin distribution, useful background when reviewing correspondent or fintech relationships with exposure to those markets. [single source]

item.title: Jeff Booth, Lyn Alden-backed ORANGE JUICE raises $40 million to launch permanent capital firm with bitcoin treasury strategy
item.description: Investors including Jeff Booth and Lyn Alden launched ORANGE JUICE, a Connecticut-based permanent capital firm that raised 40 million US dollars to buy small American businesses and reinvest their cash flow into bitcoin holdings. So what: The spread of investment vehicles that convert operating cash flow into bitcoin holdings is context for source-of-wealth and treasury-exposure assessments when such firms appear as clients or counterparties. [single source]

item.title: Tether invests $20 million in Argentine neobank Ualá as it expands Latin America push
item.description: Stablecoin issuer Tether invested 20 million US dollars for a stake of roughly 0.6 percent in Uala, an Argentine digital bank with more than 11 million customers, as part of a wider expansion across Latin America. So what: A stablecoin issuer taking equity stakes in regulated retail banks means ownership-structure and group-risk reviews of banking counterparties may increasingly need to assess stablecoin-issuer shareholders and their reserve transparency. [single source]

item.title: U.S. adds four Iran central bank crypto wallets to sanctions, Tether freezes $131 million of contents
item.description: The United States Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) added four cryptocurrency wallet addresses linked to the Central Bank of Iran to its sanctions list, and stablecoin issuer Tether froze 131 million US dollars held in them. So what: Sanctions-screening systems should pick up the newly designated wallet addresses promptly, and exposure reviews should extend beyond direct dealings to indirect flows through the liquidity providers and payment processors that serviced the wallets. [corroborated]

item.title: Visa says stablecoins will power micro-commerce in AI agentic economy
item.description: Payments network Visa, with analytics firm Artemis, published a research report arguing that stablecoins will handle very small machine-to-machine payments in an economy of artificial-intelligence agents, while card networks keep larger consumer purchases. So what: The report flags that existing legal and dispute-resolution frameworks were not designed for payments delegated to artificial-intelligence agents, an emerging control gap to watch as stablecoin-based payment products reach mainstream bank customers. [single source]

item.title: Ether outruns bitcoin as ETF money returns, almost all of from BlackRock's fund
item.description: Exchange-traded funds holding ether drew 96 million US dollars of inflows over three days, almost all into BlackRock's products, while bitcoin funds swung between large daily outflows and inflows. So what: No direct compliance action follows from these fund flows, though the concentration of institutional demand in a single asset manager's exchange-traded products is useful context for counterparty and market-risk monitoring. [single source]

item.title: OFAC Sanctions Iran Central Bank Crypto Wallets, Freezing $131M in Stablecoins
item.description: Blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis published an analysis of the United States Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) designation of four Central Bank of Iran cryptocurrency wallets, tracing how the wallets received funds before issuer Tether froze 131 million US dollars of its stablecoin. So what: The tracing links the sanctioned wallets to an institutional liquidity provider and an Asia-based payment processor, reinforcing that sanctions-exposure reviews should cover indirect relationships with such intermediaries as well as the listed addresses themselves. [corroborated]

item.title: Upbit parent Dunamu faces sanction process nearly eight months after $30 million hack: report
item.description: South Korea's Financial Supervisory Service has taken the first formal step in a sanction process against Dunamu, operator of the cryptocurrency exchange Upbit, nearly eight months after roughly 30 million US dollars of assets were stolen from the exchange. So what: The case tests how South Korea's virtual-asset user-protection law applies to exchange security failures, a precedent relevant to due diligence on exchange counterparties and to incident-accountability expectations across Asian markets. [single source]

item.title: The GENIUS Act turns 1: State of Crypto
item.description: One year after the United States enacted the GENIUS Act, its first federal stablecoin law, banking regulators are still developing the implementing rules, with proposals covering custody, capital, liquidity and customer due-diligence checks for stablecoin issuers out for comment but none yet final. So what: The direction of United States stablecoin rulemaking, including proposed know-your-customer obligations on issuers, will shape the regulatory standing of the stablecoins most used by clients and counterparties in Asia, so the finalisation timetable is worth tracking. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin's biggest advocate, Michael Saylor, says new plan to clean up the blockchain is 'a bad idea'
item.description: Michael Saylor, a prominent bitcoin advocate, publicly opposed Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 110, a proposed one-year change to the network's rules that would restrict storing arbitrary data on the bitcoin blockchain, arguing it threatens the network's neutrality. So what: The debate carries no direct regulatory implication, but a contested change to bitcoin's core rules can create network-split risk that would complicate custody, settlement and asset-listing decisions across the industry. [single source]

item.title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
item.description: Analysis of the United States GENIUS Act stablecoin law indicates Tether has until around July 2028 to restructure its stablecoin's reserves into cash and Treasury bills and register with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, or United States platforms will have to stop offering it. So what: If the largest stablecoin cannot satisfy United States requirements by 2028, liquidity and counterparty exposure could shift across venues worldwide, so the issuer's reserve restructuring and registration progress belongs in ongoing stablecoin risk assessments. [single source]

item.title: Kraken says simpler options can unlock crypto's next derivatives market
item.description: Cryptocurrency exchange Kraken launched cash-settled bitcoin and ether options priced in US dollars on its professional platform for eligible international clients, saying simpler product design can broaden the crypto derivatives market. So what: Broader retail access to crypto derivatives at global exchanges is relevant context for exchange counterparty reviews and for anticipating how regulators in Hong Kong and other Asian markets approach suitability and conduct standards for such products. [single source]

item.title: Inside Zcash's new node that targets Visa-scale privacy at 50,000 transactions per second
item.description: Independent developers released Zakura, new software for running the privacy-focused Zcash network, as part of an effort to scale private transactions from roughly one per second today toward 50,000 per second. So what: Greater scalability for privacy-preserving cryptocurrencies is relevant to transaction-monitoring and asset-listing policies, since shielded transfers limit the tracing on which anti-money-laundering controls rely. [single source]

item.title: US regulators miss GENIUS Act’s one-year deadline for final stablecoin rules
item.description: United States regulators missed the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins (GENIUS) Act's one-year deadline to finalise federal stablecoin rules, leaving reserve, capital, custody, customer-identification and anti-money-laundering rule packages at the proposal stage while the law's January 2027 effective date stands. So what: Stablecoin-exposed compliance programmes face a compressed adjustment window, as final United States reserve, custody and anti-money-laundering requirements may now arrive only shortly before the law's January 2027 effective date. [single source]

item.title: Bank of America taps new leaders to bridge crypto, AI and traditional finance
item.description: Bank of America appointed Sonali Theisen to lead its global digital-assets platform, covering tokenised deposits, stablecoins, digital collateral, trading settlement and custody, alongside a new head of artificial-intelligence transformation. So what: Digital-asset platform build-outs at major United States banks signal that tokenised deposits and stablecoin services are moving into mainstream banking, so correspondent and counterparty reviews will increasingly need to understand peer institutions' cryptoasset activities. [single source]

item.title: CryptoQuant says Strategy still needs disciplined bitcoin buying and selling framework
item.description: Research firm CryptoQuant said Strategy, the largest corporate holder of bitcoin, still lacks a systematic model for timing bitcoin purchases and a disciplined framework for selling, despite the capital-management framework the company announced in late June. So what: Concentrated corporate bitcoin treasuries carry liquidity and governance risks that remain relevant when risk-assessing clients or counterparties whose balance sheets depend heavily on cryptoasset holdings. [single source]

item.title: Stripe and Swift race to control the next generation of global payments infrastructure
item.description: Payments firm Stripe has launched an unsolicited 53 billion United States dollar bid for PayPal, whose platform includes a dollar stablecoin, while the interbank messaging network Swift is expanding a blockchain-based settlement initiative to more than 40 financial institutions. So what: Consolidation of stablecoin and blockchain settlement capability inside the largest payment networks would concentrate transaction flows that sanctions-screening and transaction-monitoring arrangements at correspondent banks will need to cover. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin slides toward $63,000 as Coinbase premium stays negative for a record 60 days
item.description: Bitcoin fell below 63,000 United States dollars as the Coinbase price premium, a gauge of United States institutional demand, stayed negative for a record 60 consecutive days. So what: This is market context rather than a control trigger, though a prolonged downturn is relevant when assessing the financial resilience of virtual-asset trading platforms and other crypto-exposed clients. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin ETFs could mirror gold’s history of ‘spectacular gains’ and ‘painful drawdowns,’ analyst says
item.description: A Bloomberg exchange-traded-fund analyst argued that bitcoin exchange-traded funds may repeat gold funds' pattern of spectacular gains followed by long drawdowns, with the largest bitcoin fund's assets down to about 60 billion United States dollars from a brief 100 billion peak. So what: Fund-flow volatility is primarily an investor-suitability matter, though the continued shift of retail cryptoasset exposure into regulated fund wrappers moves activity into channels with established intermediary oversight. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin faces fresh headwinds as China’s Kimi beats Claude, GPT in coding benchmark
item.description: Bitcoin came under pressure after Beijing-based Moonshot AI released an open-weight model that topped United States rivals on a coding benchmark, unsettling the artificial-intelligence data-centre business model that many bitcoin miners have adopted. So what: There is no regulatory action here; the episode mainly illustrates how closely cryptoasset prices now track the artificial-intelligence investment cycle, a market linkage rather than a financial-crime-compliance issue. [single source]

item.title: Airbnb CEO says X account was hacked, attacker posted AI-slop on tokenization
item.description: Airbnb's chief executive said his account on the social-media platform X was hacked, with the attacker posting artificial-intelligence-generated promotional threads about tokenising real-world assets, though no scam links or wallet addresses were posted. So what: Compromised high-profile social-media accounts remain a standard opening move in cryptoasset investment scams, reinforcing customer-education and fraud-typology material warning that authentic-looking endorsements of token products can be fake. [single source]

item.title: Taiwan sentences BitShine crypto exchange ringleader to 22 years in $39 million fraud case: reports
item.description: A Taiwanese court sentenced the ringleader of the BitShine cryptocurrency-exchange fraud to 22 years in prison for defrauding about 1,539 victims of roughly 39 million United States dollars and laundering larger sums overseas via the stablecoin Tether. So what: The group hired compliance staff to build legitimate-looking customer checks and then coached members to bypass them, underlining that polished onboarding paperwork at regional virtual-asset platforms is not reliable evidence of genuine anti-money-laundering controls. [corroborated]

item.title: UK gang jailed over $5.4 million crypto scam posing as police
item.description: A London court jailed three men who posed as police officers and used fake police websites to steal about 4 million pounds (5.4 million United States dollars) in cryptocurrency from eight victims, following a Metropolitan Police investigation. So what: Police-impersonation scams that trick victims into surrendering cryptocurrency credentials are an active typology, and customer transfers prompted by purported law-enforcement contact remain a red flag worth reflecting in fraud and transaction-monitoring scenarios. [corroborated]

item.title: USDT vs USDC: Comparing the Two Largest Stablecoins
item.description: An educational explainer compares the two largest dollar-pegged stablecoins, Tether (USDT) and USD Coin (USDC), on market share, reserves, transparency and regulatory standing, noting USD Coin complies with the European Union's Markets in Crypto-Assets framework while Tether does not meet new United States stablecoin-law requirements. So what: Reserve transparency and regulatory standing differ materially between the two dominant stablecoins, which matters when risk-rating client flows, since tokens restricted in some markets can carry heightened regulatory and counterparty risk. [single source]

item.title: Grayscale could take Worldcoin to Wall Street after ETF filing with the SEC
item.description: Asset manager Grayscale filed a registration statement with the United States Securities and Exchange Commission for an exchange-traded fund that would hold Worldcoin, the token of the eye-scanning digital-identity project co-founded by OpenAI's chief executive. So what: The steady extension of United States exchange-traded funds to smaller, novel tokens widens the set of cryptoassets reachable through mainstream brokerage channels, gradually expanding what product-approval and screening processes may need to cover. [single source]

item.title: Trump’s crypto ethics stance leaves Clarity Act negotiations in limbo
item.description: Negotiations over the Clarity Act, the proposed United States law that would set a market-structure framework for cryptoassets, remain stalled because President Trump has not agreed to ethics language restricting federal officials' profits from cryptoassets, with a Senate vote targeted for early August. So what: Delay to the main United States market-structure bill prolongs regulatory uncertainty for cryptoasset counterparties with United States exposure, leaving current rules as the operative baseline for counterparty and product assessments ahead of a possible Senate vote in early August. [single source]

item.title: Vietnam sets new crypto trading fines on par with drunk-driving penalties
item.description: Vietnam's government issued a decree, taking effect on 1 September 2026, that fines individuals and organisations for trading cryptoassets outside newly licensed domestic exchanges, with penalties for unlicensed service providers of up to about 200 million Vietnamese dong (roughly 7,600 United States dollars). So what: Vietnam is drawing a hard licensing perimeter around cryptoasset trading, so country-risk assessments and transaction-monitoring assumptions for Vietnamese retail and corporate flows to offshore exchanges will need updating once the penalties take effect in September. [corroborated]

item.title: Where crypto fits in the FATF’s new public-private partnership report
item.description: Analytics firm Elliptic reviewed a new Financial Action Task Force (FATF) report on public-private partnerships against financial crime, which finds the cryptoasset industry remains under-integrated into government-industry information-sharing arrangements. So what: With the Financial Action Task Force pressing for deeper public-private information sharing under the new United Kingdom presidency, banks active in digital assets can expect encouragement to bring cryptoasset expertise and blockchain analytics into existing intelligence-sharing partnerships, including in Hong Kong. [single source]

item.title: Saylor's Strategy raises cash reserves to $3.2 billion, leaving bitcoin holdings unchanged
item.description: Strategy, the largest corporate holder of bitcoin, raised its cash reserves to about 3.2 billion United States dollars through share sales while leaving its roughly 843,775 bitcoin untouched, rebuilding a buffer to support dividends on its preferred stock after recent market pressure on its financing model. So what: The episode illustrates how leveraged corporate bitcoin-treasury structures can come under funding strain in downturns, a concentration and credit consideration when assessing exposure to counterparties whose balance sheets are dominated by cryptoassets. [single source]

item.title: Brazil’s securities regulator sets up task force with 60-day deadline for tokenization proposal
item.description: Brazil's securities regulator established a working group, due to report within 60 days of its installation, to propose an experimental framework for issuing, trading and settling tokenised securities, covering questions such as official ownership records, private-key custody and transaction reversibility. So what: Another sizeable market designing rules for tokenised securities adds to the fragmented international landscape that compliance functions supporting custody and tokenisation business must track, and Brazil's answers on private-key custody and transaction reversibility may be cited in policy debates elsewhere. [single source]

item.title: Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin ‘vibe-codes’ anonymous billboard demo with onchain moderation on Aztec
item.description: Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin released a demonstration application on the privacy-focused Aztec network that lets users post messages with no public link to their identity or original deposits, moderated by an automated system whose rules are stored transparently on the blockchain. So what: Privacy systems that sever the link between deposits and subsequent on-chain activity illustrate the limits of blockchain-tracing tools, a factor to weigh when risk-rating exposure to privacy-focused networks in transaction monitoring and source-of-funds reviews. [single source]

item.title: Cross-chain protocol Allbridge halts after $1.65 million flash loan exploit
item.description: Cross-chain bridge protocol Allbridge paused operations after an attacker used a flash loan — a large uncollateralised loan borrowed and repaid within a single transaction — to distort its Solana stablecoin pools and extract about 1.65 million United States dollars. So what: The stolen funds were quickly bridged to Ethereum and dispersed across addresses, a reminder that exploit proceeds move fast across chains and that exposure screening should pick up addresses linked to newly reported bridge incidents. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin ETFs see new money again, but inflows remain ‘peanuts’ relative to the recent exodus
item.description: United States-listed spot bitcoin exchange-traded funds recorded about 273 million United States dollars of net inflows over two weeks, ending an eight-week withdrawal streak in which more than 8 billion United States dollars had left the products. So what: Fund-flow swings of this scale mainly signal shifting institutional sentiment rather than any compliance obligation, though sustained outflows from exchange-traded products can foreshadow reduced client activity in related brokerage and custody services. [single source]

item.title: Allbridge Core pauses protocol after $1.65 million flash loan exploit: onchain analysts
item.description: Cross-chain bridge protocol Allbridge Core was paused after a flash-loan attack manipulated its stablecoin pools for a loss of about 1.65 million United States dollars, with security firms tracking the stolen funds as they moved through privacy tools from Solana to Ethereum. So what: The routing of bridge-exploit proceeds through privacy protocols within hours underlines why screening tools need timely incident-attribution data, particularly for funds arriving from cross-chain bridges. [single source]

item.title: Stablecoins vs Traditional Banking
item.description: An educational explainer contrasts stablecoins with traditional bank deposits, noting that under the United States' GENIUS Act stablecoin issuers must hold full reserves and may not pay interest to holders, while banks retain deposit insurance, lending functions and newly chartered stablecoin competitors under federal supervision. So what: The piece is background reading rather than a new development, but the distinction it draws — full-reserve, non-interest-bearing stablecoins versus insured bank deposits — frames how client questions about stablecoin exposure are likely to arise as United States-regulated issuers expand. [single source]

item.title: Saylor urges Bitcoin to reject BIP-110 in 110-point essay as soft fork’s August showdown approaches
item.description: Michael Saylor, co-founder of bitcoin-holding company Strategy, published an essay opposing Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 110 (BIP-110), a contested temporary change to Bitcoin's rules that would restrict data-heavy transactions, ahead of a miner signalling period expected to begin in early August. So what: A contested change to Bitcoin's consensus rules carries some risk of a network split, an operational scenario that custody and settlement functions typically prepare for with deposit-freeze and fork-handling procedures. [single source]

item.title: The next blind spot in financial crime compliance: Crypto and fiat convergence
item.description: An opinion article in an anti-money-laundering trade publication argues that the growing convergence of cryptocurrency and traditional (fiat) money and payment systems is creating a new blind spot for financial-crime-compliance programmes. So what: As value moves more fluidly between cryptocurrency and conventional banking channels, financial-crime-compliance functions that monitor the two separately risk missing laundering activity that crosses between them. [single source]

item.title: Crypto Clarity Act still at mercy of ethics section as Democrats balk at Trump deal
item.description: The United States Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, a major bill setting the regulatory framework for cryptocurrency markets, remains stalled in the Senate because Democrats object to a plan for the United States Department of Justice, rather than state attorneys general, to enforce new ethics rules restricting government officials' cryptocurrency holdings. So what: The final shape and timing of comprehensive United States cryptocurrency market rules remain uncertain, so compliance functions cannot yet plan definitively around how United States-facing digital-asset activity will be supervised. [single source]

item.title: Trump-backed crypto ethics rule puts DOJ in charge of enforcement, prohibits federal officials from issuing cryptocurrencies: sources
item.description: According to unnamed sources, President Trump has agreed to ethics language in the United States cryptocurrency market-structure bill (the Clarity Act) that would bar federal officials, including members of Congress, the president and vice-president, from issuing their own cryptocurrencies, with the United States Department of Justice responsible for enforcement rather than state attorneys general. So what: The enforcement mechanism and terms of United States cryptocurrency market legislation remain contested, leaving the eventual United States compliance framework for digital assets unsettled. [single source]

item.title: The Digital Chamber sues Illinois over incoming crypto transaction tax
item.description: A cryptocurrency industry trade association, the Digital Chamber, filed a lawsuit challenging a new Illinois state law that would levy a 0.2% tax on digital-asset transactions from January 2027, arguing the tax unfairly singles out digital assets. So what: State-level taxes on digital-asset transactions could add cost and reporting complexity to United States cryptocurrency activity, and this challenge signals that such treatment remains legally unsettled across individual United States states. [single source]

item.title: Satsuma shareholders approve bitcoin treasury liquidation and London delisting
item.description: Shareholders of Satsuma Technology, a United Kingdom-listed company that had adopted a strategy of holding bitcoin on its balance sheet, voted to sell those holdings, return the cash to investors, and remove the company from the London Stock Exchange. So what: The unwinding of a listed company's bitcoin-treasury strategy illustrates the balance-sheet and valuation risks such structures carry, which is relevant when assessing credit or counterparty exposure to firms that hold large amounts of cryptocurrency. [single source]

item.title: RippleX sees XRP Ledger agentic transactions hitting 10-million mark soon
item.description: RippleX, the developer arm of the company behind the XRP Ledger blockchain, said payments initiated automatically by artificial-intelligence software agents on that network have passed one million and could, in its projection, reach ten million or more within a couple of years. So what: The growth of autonomous, artificial-intelligence-initiated blockchain payments raises new questions for transaction monitoring and customer-due-diligence controls, because value can move without a person directly authorising each transfer. [single source]

item.title: White House pushes Senate Democrats to take 'historic' crypto Clarity Act ethics deal
item.description: The White House is pressing Senate Democrats to accept President Trump's proposed ethics provisions in the United States cryptocurrency market-structure bill (the Clarity Act), which it calls historic but whose details it has not disclosed, while several Democratic senators remain skeptical of relying on the United States Department of Justice to enforce them. So what: Ongoing political disagreement over the United States cryptocurrency market-structure bill keeps the timing and final content of United States digital-asset regulation uncertain for compliance planning. [single source]

item.title: Solana, Hyperliquid ETFs dominate altcoin fund flows despite flying under the radar
item.description: Exchange-traded funds (investment funds whose shares trade like stocks) holding the cryptocurrencies Solana and Hyperliquid have attracted large amounts of investor money, reported at roughly $904 million and $350 million respectively, despite receiving less attention than bitcoin and ether funds. So what: Growing investor demand for funds tracking smaller, higher-volatility cryptocurrencies broadens the range of digital-asset exposure reaching mainstream investment channels, which is relevant to monitoring client exposure and product-suitability considerations. [single source]

item.title: Russia passes landmark crypto bill allowing regulated retail trading
item.description: Russia's lower house of parliament, the State Duma, passed a bill to legalise and regulate cryptocurrency trading by retail investors, imposing annual purchase limits for non-professional investors, knowledge tests, and a mandatory registry for exchanges, custodians and brokers, although the measure still needs upper-house approval and the president's signature and domestic cryptocurrency payments would remain banned. So what: A regulated Russian retail-cryptocurrency market with a licensed-intermediary registry creates new sanctions- and jurisdiction-risk considerations, given existing international restrictions on Russia, when screening cryptocurrency exchanges and counterparties. [corroborated]

item.title: Augustus raises $180 million Series B at $1 billion valuation to build the ‘Global Dollar Bank’
item.description: Augustus, a United States financial-technology startup building a federally chartered 'Global Dollar Bank' that would give international financial firms direct access to United States dollar accounts and payments using both stablecoins and traditional banking rails, raised $180 million in funding at a $1 billion valuation. So what: A stablecoin-integrated bank offering international firms direct United States dollar access could become a new channel for cross-border dollar flows, warranting attention to how such providers apply customer-due-diligence and transaction-monitoring controls. [single source]

item.title: Bitget plans US expansion ‘with or without’ Clarity Act, CEO says
item.description: Gracy Chen, chief executive of the offshore cryptocurrency exchange Bitget, said the company plans to enter the United States market and obtain money-transmitter, derivatives and broker-dealer licences regardless of whether the pending United States cryptocurrency market-structure bill (the Clarity Act) passes. So what: An offshore exchange seeking United States licences reflects continued migration of cryptocurrency venues toward regulated status, which is relevant when assessing venue and counterparty risk for such platforms. [single source]

item.title: Uphold launches fractional share trading in US for more than 4,000 stocks and ETFs
item.description: Uphold, a cryptocurrency trading platform, launched fractional-share trading in the United States, letting customers buy and sell more than 4,000 United States stocks and exchange-traded funds, including switching directly between cryptocurrency and shares, through its regulated United States broker-dealer and money-services entities. So what: Platforms that let customers move seamlessly between cryptocurrency and traditional securities blur the line between the two asset classes, which is relevant to transaction monitoring and to understanding how client funds flow between digital and conventional markets. [single source]

item.title: Coinbase says ‘low-risk’ config change behind 50-minute July 14 outage impacting trading, card transactions
item.description: The cryptocurrency exchange Coinbase said a roughly 50-minute outage on 14 July 2026, which halted trading, deposits, withdrawals and Coinbase Card transactions, was caused by an unintended misconfiguration during a routine update to its internal systems, and stated that customer funds were never at risk. So what: A leading exchange's operational failure underscores concentration and operational-resilience risk in cryptocurrency market infrastructure, which is relevant when relying on such venues for settlement, custody or client transaction flows. [single source]

item.title: Russia’s parliament passes crypto market law with $3,800 annual cap for retail investors
item.description: Russia's State Duma, the lower house of parliament, passed a bill setting up the country's first comprehensive framework for regulating cryptocurrency exchanges and other service providers — capping ordinary retail investors' annual crypto purchases at about 3,800 US dollars per licensed intermediary, requiring exchanges and brokers to join an official registry, and permitting digital currencies for settling foreign-trade contracts between Russian and non-Russian parties — with most provisions due to take effect from 1 September 2026 once the bill clears the upper house and receives the president's signature. So what: Because the bill would permit cryptocurrency for cross-border trade settlement in a heavily sanctioned economy once it takes effect, it is worth tracking as a potential sanctions- and export-control-evasion channel, and the planned registry of licensed Russian exchanges is relevant when assessing counterparty and payment-screening risk. [corroborated]

item.title: Augustus raises $180 million to build a clearing bank for the AI and stablecoin era
item.description: Augustus, a fintech startup that has won conditional US approval to operate as a national bank, raised 180 million US dollars — led by investment firm Tiger Global at a 1 billion dollar valuation — to build a 'clearing bank' offering round-the-clock settlement infrastructure that links traditional payment systems with blockchain networks for the stablecoin era. So what: The funding signals continued institutionalisation of stablecoin settlement infrastructure under a prospective US bank charter, worth monitoring as regulated stablecoin payment rails mature into potential correspondents or counterparties. [single source]

item.title: Jack Mallers leaves Twenty One as Strike exits Tether’s three-way bitcoin merger
item.description: Jack Mallers stepped down as chief executive of Twenty One Capital, a publicly traded company that holds bitcoin as a treasury asset, and his payments firm Strike pulled out of a three-way merger that stablecoin issuer Tether had proposed to combine Twenty One, Strike and a bitcoin-mining business. So what: The reshuffle is corporate and market news with no direct compliance action, relevant mainly as background when assessing the stability and structure of bitcoin-treasury and crypto-payment firms that may appear as clients or counterparties. [single source]

item.title: Crypto regulatory affairs: a new regulatory future in the age of AI
item.description: A blockchain-analytics firm's blog surveys emerging trends in cryptocurrency regulation, noting that regulators in Dubai, the United Kingdom, Hong Kong and Singapore are starting to use artificial intelligence for real-time supervision, alongside developments such as the Financial Action Task Force's progress on its 'travel rule' (which requires firms to share sender and recipient details for crypto transfers) and closer US-UK coordination on stablecoin regulation. So what: The round-up points to a supervisory shift toward artificial-intelligence-enabled, real-time oversight and continued roll-out of crypto information-sharing requirements, trends that virtual-asset compliance functions should track as regulators' expectations evolve. [single source]

item.title: Jack Mallers steps down as XXI Capital CEO as Tether's plans to merge three bitcoin firms falls
item.description: Jack Mallers stepped down as chief executive of Twenty One Capital (also styled XXI), and stablecoin issuer Tether's plan to merge three of its bitcoin-focused companies — Twenty One, the Strike payments firm and a bitcoin miner — collapsed, with Strike staying independent and Raphael Zagury appointed as the new chief executive. So what: The leadership change and abandoned merger are corporate news with no direct compliance action, useful only as background on the stability and structure of bitcoin-treasury and crypto-payment firms that could appear as clients or counterparties. [single source]

item.title: UK Parliament begins inquiry into banking chokepoint for crypto businesses
item.description: A cross-party group of United Kingdom parliamentarians launched an inquiry, with a six-week call for written evidence, into why banks routinely refuse or restrict banking services for cryptocurrency businesses — a practice often called 'debanking'. So what: Rising political scrutiny of banks' crypto-sector 'debanking' signals that account-closure and de-risking decisions on virtual-asset clients may face closer examination, relevant to how such risk-appetite and exit decisions are documented and justified. [single source]

item.title: Screening HTX Beyond the UK Designation: Why Blockchain Intelligence Matters
item.description: A blockchain-analytics firm analyses how the cryptocurrency exchange HTX (formerly Huobi) has responded to the United Kingdom's May 2026 sanctions designation of its operator — for allegedly moving about 1.5 billion US dollars for Russia-aligned entities — describing how the exchange continually rotates its wallet addresses across several blockchains to evade fixed screening blocklists. So what: A major exchange with deep Asian roots continuing to operate after a United Kingdom sanctions designation, while shifting wallet addresses to defeat static blocklists, highlights that effective sanctions screening of virtual-asset flows may require behaviour-based methods rather than fixed address lists alone. [corroborated]

item.title: FinCEN Extends Comment Period for Proposed Rule To Amend the Definition of Huione Group
item.description: The United States Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) pushed back its public-comment deadline to 2 August 2026 on a proposed rule that would broaden the official definition of the Huione Group — a Southeast Asian network FinCEN has designated a primary money-laundering concern — to also cover an affiliated payment company and any successor, after a technical fault blocked electronic comment filing for several days. So what: The widening scope of names tied to this money-laundering network expands what correspondent-banking and transaction-screening controls should capture, and the reopened comment window until early August gives affected institutions a further chance to respond before the rule is finalised. [official]

item.title: Bipartisan support ‘critical’ as Democrats push back on GOP crypto bill on ethics grounds
item.description: United States Senate Democrats objected to Senate Republicans' newly released draft of the CLARITY Act — the main federal bill to set market-structure rules for digital assets — arguing it lacks adequate safeguards against conflicts of interest, including those linked to President Trump's crypto ventures, and warning it cannot pass without stronger bipartisan support. So what: The disputed timing and content of the main US digital-asset market-structure bill mean that firms planning for a future US framework covering registration, custody and stablecoins should treat its final shape and timing as still unsettled. [single source]

item.title: Tesla holds bitcoin treasury steady, reports $112M impairment loss
item.description: Tesla left its bitcoin holdings unchanged at 11,509 coins during the second quarter of 2026 but booked a 112 million US dollar after-tax impairment charge as bitcoin's price fell during the period, under accounting rules that require companies to record declines in digital-asset value through earnings. So what: The charge is a reminder that corporate bitcoin holdings introduce earnings volatility under current digital-asset accounting rules, useful context when assessing corporate clients or counterparties that carry cryptocurrency on their balance sheets. [single source]

item.title: Clarity Act’s impact on Circle could be negative over the long term, Mizuho says
item.description: Analysts at the bank Mizuho said the proposed US CLARITY Act — a bill that would set clearer rules for digital-asset markets — could hurt Circle, the company behind the USDC dollar-pegged stablecoin, over the long term, because clearer stablecoin rules would draw in more competitors and squeeze Circle's revenue, and they kept a below-market price target on its shares. So what: The view that a clearer US stablecoin framework would intensify competition among issuers is relevant when weighing the durability and concentration of the stablecoin issuers whose tokens may appear in customer flows. [single source]

item.title: Kraken parent Payward partners with GTN to scale xStocks internationally, beyond US equities
item.description: Payward, the parent of cryptocurrency exchange Kraken, partnered with financial-infrastructure firm GTN to expand xStocks — a service that issues blockchain-based tokens representing shares and exchange-traded funds — into more than 90 markets, beginning with Hong Kong-listed equities and then extending to the United Kingdom, Europe and South Korea. So what: A tokenised-equities platform launching first with Hong Kong-listed stocks underscores the growing tokenisation of traditional securities in the region, relevant to how tokenised real-world assets, their custody and settlement may enter client portfolios and require combined securities and anti-money-laundering controls. [corroborated]

item.title: SEC reaches settlement with Coinbase over records requests from Gensler era
item.description: The United States Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) settled a lawsuit brought by cryptocurrency exchange Coinbase over the agency's handling of Freedom of Information Act records requests dating to former chair Gary Gensler's era, agreeing to pay 150,000 US dollars in fees, release two withheld documents and review its records- and text-message-preservation practices. So what: The settlement concerns US regulators' records-transparency practices rather than crypto rules themselves, carrying limited operational impact but signalling continued scrutiny of how US agencies documented their oversight of the crypto industry. [single source]

item.title: Senate releases latest version of Clarity Act including software developer protections and ethics provision with ‘sunset’ date in 2029
item.description: The United States Senate released a new draft of the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act — the main bill to set market rules for cryptocurrencies — adding legal protections for non-custodial software developers and a temporary ethics provision, expiring in 2029, that would bar federal officials and their spouses from issuing or sponsoring their own digital tokens. So what: A near-final United States market-structure law would redraw which federal agency oversees which digital assets, informing how United States-facing crypto businesses and counterparties are classified and supervised. [single source]

item.title: New draft of Clarity Act is out, and it would impose limits on Trump's crypto empire
item.description: A new Senate draft of the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, the main United States bill to regulate cryptocurrency markets, includes an ethics provision that would restrict the president and senior officials from issuing or profiting from their own digital tokens, with the restriction set to expire in 2029. So what: Continued progress toward a United States market-structure law signals a firmer regulatory perimeter for digital assets, relevant when assessing the regulatory standing of United States-based crypto counterparties. [single source]

item.title: Crypto ‘wrench attacks’ persist as total climbs to 52 and value targeted jumps more than tenfold, CertiK says
item.description: The blockchain-security firm CertiK reported 52 physical 'wrench attacks' — incidents where criminals use violence or threats to force cryptocurrency holders to hand over their assets — in the first half of 2026, with the total value targeted rising more than tenfold to roughly 124 million United States dollars and most incidents occurring in Europe. So what: A sharp rise in violent coercion of cryptocurrency holders is a personal-security and extortion-risk indicator relevant to protecting high-net-worth clients and to monitoring for extortion-linked movement of funds. [single source]

item.title: SEC's Peirce warns some DeFi vaults, onchain lending may fall under securities laws
item.description: A United States Securities and Exchange Commission commissioner, Hester Peirce, warned that some decentralised-finance 'vaults' and on-chain lending arrangements — software that automatically pools and invests users' crypto — may qualify as securities under United States law depending on how they are managed. So what: A senior regulator signalling that automated on-chain investment products can fall within securities law bears on how such products, and the platforms offering them, are classified and risk-rated when they surface in client or counterparty activity. [single source]

item.title: ‘You will have a painful fall’: SEC’s Peirce warns crypto vault builders against twisting securities law
item.description: A United States Securities and Exchange Commission commissioner, Hester Peirce, cautioned builders of crypto 'vaults' against contorting securities law to avoid it, saying on-chain investment products and lending can still count as securities and that those attempting to evade the rules will 'have a painful fall.' So what: The regulator's warning that moving investment activity on-chain does not by itself escape securities law is relevant to assessing the compliance posture of decentralised-finance products and their operators. [single source]

item.title: Benchmark lowers Coinbase earnings estimates but says Clarity Act could eclipse weak second quarter
item.description: The research firm Benchmark lowered its earnings estimates for the United States crypto exchange Coinbase after a weak second quarter of trading but kept a 'buy' recommendation, arguing that the pending Clarity Act crypto legislation could outweigh the soft results. So what: Analyst earnings commentary on a listed crypto exchange carries little direct financial-crime-compliance action, though it underscores that the pending United States market-structure bill is viewed as decisive for exchange business models. [single source]

item.title: ‘Optimistic signals’ meet a $68,000 wall as bitcoin’s recovery faces its next test: analysts
item.description: Market analysts said bitcoin's recovery to around 66,000 United States dollars faces resistance near 68,000 United States dollars — a level tied to the average purchase price of recent buyers — and were divided on whether the rally can continue. So what: Bitcoin price commentary carries no direct financial-crime-compliance action, though sustained price and flow shifts can influence the overall crypto transaction volumes seen in monitoring. [single source]

item.title: How Bitcoin ATM scams work and how banks can spot the risk on-chain
item.description: The blockchain-analytics firm Elliptic published an explainer on how criminals exploit Bitcoin cash machines — kiosks that convert cash into cryptocurrency — to defraud victims, often older people, and how bank staff can recognise the warning signs and trace the stolen funds on the blockchain. So what: The described pattern — a customer withdrawing cash and depositing it at a cryptocurrency machine at a stranger's direction — supplies concrete red flags for branch and transaction-monitoring staff and for tracing scam proceeds across the blockchain. [single source]

item.title: U.S. seeks forfeiture of $25 million in crypto tied to romance and investment scams
item.description: United States federal prosecutors filed five civil forfeiture complaints seeking to recover more than 25 million United States dollars in cryptocurrency linked to international romance and investment scams, part of a task force that has recovered over 800 million United States dollars since November 2025. So what: A continued stream of United States forfeiture actions against scam proceeds reinforces the value of monitoring for romance- and investment-fraud typologies and may surface wallet addresses and counterparties worth screening. [corroborated]

item.title: Midnight token rebounds 19% after Wanchain bridge hack, Hoskinson calls for ZK revamp
item.description: The NIGHT token tied to the Midnight blockchain project rebounded about 19% after earlier crashing when attackers exploited the Wanchain cross-chain 'bridge' and drained 290 million tokens, prompting Cardano founder Charles Hoskinson to call for an industry overhaul using privacy-focused zero-knowledge technology. So what: Recurring exploits of cross-chain bridges — the software linking different blockchains — remain a leading source of stolen crypto and a laundering channel, relevant when assessing exposure to bridge-linked tokens and tracing illicit cross-chain flows. [single source]

item.title: Kraken parent expands tokenized stocks to Hong Kong, UK and South Korea equities
item.description: Payward, the parent company of the crypto exchange Kraken, said it will expand its tokenised-stock platform — which issues blockchain-based versions of company shares — to include Hong Kong, United Kingdom and South Korean equities, with Hong Kong-listed stocks launching first, subject to regulatory approvals. So what: The extension of tokenised equities into Hong Kong and other markets raises cross-border questions on securities classification, custody and distribution that are relevant to how such instruments would be treated if they appear in client portfolios or counterparty offerings. [corroborated]

item.title: Balance stablecoin collapses 99% after $1 million exploit drains its bitcoin vaults
item.description: Balance, a small algorithmic stablecoin meant to hold a value of one United States dollar, collapsed more than 99% after an attacker manipulated the price feed it relied on and drained roughly 900,000 United States dollars from its bitcoin-backed reserves. So what: The episode illustrates how price-feed (oracle) manipulation can break a stablecoin's peg, a design-risk typology worth understanding when evaluating exposure to smaller or algorithmic stablecoins. [single source]

item.title: BIS warns USD stablecoins can evade capital controls, challenging traditional market regulations
item.description: The Bank for International Settlements, the Basel-based body that serves central banks, published a study finding that United States dollar stablecoins largely bypass national capital controls and foreign-exchange restrictions across more than 130 economies, warning this could accelerate hard-to-reverse dollarisation in developing markets. So what: A finding from a leading international standard-setter that dollar stablecoins can evade capital controls is directly relevant to jurisdictions with strict controls, including mainland China, and to assessing stablecoin-related cross-border flow and sanctions-evasion risk. [corroborated]

item.title: US seeks forfeiture of $25 million in crypto tied to global fraud investigations
item.description: The United States Attorney's Office for the District of Columbia has moved to seize more than 25 million United States dollars in cryptocurrency traced to a series of international investment-fraud, online romance and fraudulent-recovery schemes, as part of a task force that has recovered over 800 million dollars. So what: The action underscores that proceeds from cross-border scam operations are routinely laundered through cryptocurrency by networks based in Southeast Asia, reinforcing the value of scam-related typology awareness and transaction monitoring for institutions exposed to such flows. [corroborated]

item.title: HTX keeps rotating wallets to evade UK sanctions screening: TRM Labs
item.description: The blockchain-analytics firm TRM Labs reported that the cryptocurrency exchange HTX, formerly known as Huobi, repeatedly rotates its wallet addresses across several blockchains every few hours, a practice that undermines address-based sanctions screening after the United Kingdom sanctioned the exchange in May 2026 over alleged Russian sanctions evasion. So what: The finding highlights the limits of static wallet-list screening against a sanctioned exchange and supports supplementing it with behaviour-based blockchain analytics when assessing exposure to sanctioned virtual-asset service providers. [corroborated]

item.title: Pakistan launches crypto investigation unit to crack down on digital asset crimes: report
item.description: Pakistan's Federal Investigation Agency has established a specialised unit to investigate the use of cryptocurrency in money laundering, terrorism financing, cybercrime and drug trafficking, operating alongside the country's newly created Pakistan Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority. So what: The move signals growing enforcement capacity in an emerging virtual-asset jurisdiction, a development worth tracking when assessing counterparty and correspondent-banking risk connected to Pakistan-based cryptocurrency activity. [single source]

item.title: Crypto lobby group Digital Chamber sues Illinois to block digital asset tax
item.description: The Digital Chamber, a cryptocurrency industry group, has filed a federal lawsuit to stop an Illinois law that would place a 0.2 percent tax on digital-asset transactions from taking effect in January 2027, arguing the measure unlawfully singles out blockchain-based finance. So what: The dispute is a United States state-tax and litigation matter with limited direct financial-crime relevance, though it illustrates the widening patchwork of state-level digital-asset rules that internationally active institutions must monitor. [single source]

item.title: The compliance powers of stablecoins
item.description: An opinion piece argues that stablecoins give compliance teams real-time visibility into transactions and the ability to freeze or recover illicit funds in ways traditional payment systems cannot, pointing to past cases in which issuers blocked or seized criminal proceeds. So what: The commentary reframes the traceability and freeze capabilities of stablecoin transactions as a compliance asset as well as a risk, useful context when designing monitoring and controls for stablecoin exposure. [single source]

item.title: Tren de Aragua, Crypto, and Cash: Inside One of Chile’s Biggest Money Laundering Cases
item.description: An analysis of one of Chile's largest money-laundering cases describes how the Tren de Aragua criminal organisation used cryptocurrency alongside cash and bank accounts to launder proceeds from drug trafficking, extortion and other crimes. So what: The case reinforces awareness of combined cryptocurrency-and-cash layering typologies used by violent transnational criminal groups, informing transaction monitoring and screening for exposure to Latin American organised-crime flows. [single source]

item.title: Robinhood CEO Vlad Tenev's X account hacked to promote token amid memecoin frenzy
item.description: Robinhood chief executive Vlad Tenev's X account, formerly Twitter, was hacked and used to promote a fake 'Vladhood' memecoin, a novelty cryptocurrency token, falsely presented as an official Robinhood token, which the company confirmed and had removed. So what: The incident is a reminder that compromised executive social-media accounts are used to lend false legitimacy to fraudulent token promotions, a social-engineering pattern relevant to fraud monitoring and customer-scam awareness. [single source]

item.title: Tassat wants to help smaller banks tap the trillion-dollar stablecoin boom before Wall Street lock them out
item.description: The financial-technology firm Tassat unveiled a platform, Project NENYA, intended to help regional and midsize United States banks compete for stablecoin-reserve deposits by linking regulated stablecoin issuers with multiple banks, following passage of the United States GENIUS Act stablecoin law. So what: The initiative reflects how new United States stablecoin legislation is drawing traditional banks toward holding stablecoin reserves, a trend relevant to understanding how deposit, custody and reserve-management risks may spread across the banking sector. [single source]

item.title: Robinhood CEO’s X account hacked to promote Vladhood memecoin flagged as ‘scam’
item.description: Robinhood chief executive Vlad Tenev's X account, formerly Twitter, was hacked to promote a 'Vladhood' novelty token that a blockchain-tracking tool flagged as a potential scam, an incident the company confirmed and said it was working to reverse. So what: The episode illustrates how attackers exploit hacked executive accounts to give fraudulent tokens a veneer of legitimacy, a social-engineering pattern relevant to fraud-monitoring and scam-awareness controls. [single source]

item.title: MoonPay now accepts Discover Network cards for crypto purchases and sales in US
item.description: The cryptocurrency-payments firm MoonPay added support for Discover Network cards, letting United States users buy and sell cryptocurrency with Discover cards for the first time, alongside the Visa and Mastercard networks it already supports. So what: Wider use of payment cards to buy and sell cryptocurrency is a consumer-payments development with limited direct financial-crime impact, though broader card-to-crypto access can raise card-not-present fraud considerations for the banks that issue those cards. [single source]

item.title: Coinbase's corporate customers can now accept payments from AI agents
item.description: Coinbase launched a feature letting its business customers accept payments in USDC, a United States dollar-pegged stablecoin, from autonomous artificial-intelligence software agents using an in-house payments protocol called x402, and released a developer toolkit to add the capability to online services. So what: Agent-initiated stablecoin payments introduce a new type of payer into transaction flows, raising forward-looking questions for know-your-customer, authorisation and transaction-monitoring frameworks as machine-driven payments emerge. [single source]

item.title: Swan CEO claims Twenty One serves Tether’s US political interests, calls Mallers’ role ‘ceremonial’
item.description: The chief executive of Swan Bitcoin publicly alleged that the bitcoin-treasury company Twenty One Capital exists mainly to serve the stablecoin issuer Tether's United States political interests and dismissed departing executive Jack Mallers' role as ceremonial, claims that Tether did not address. So what: The dispute is corporate and political commentary among industry executives with no direct compliance action, though the ownership links between a major stablecoin issuer and its United States corporate vehicles are worth noting when mapping stablecoin-issuer group structures. [single source]

item.title: Coinbase calls AI payments its ‘most high-conviction bet’ as businesses begin accepting agent payments
item.description: Coinbase described enabling businesses to accept payments from autonomous artificial-intelligence software agents as one of its highest-conviction strategic bets, using its Coinbase Payments service and the x402 open payments standard to let agents pay in USDC, a United States dollar-pegged stablecoin. So what: The strategic emphasis on machine-initiated stablecoin payments signals that agent-driven payment flows may grow, prompting forward-looking review of how know-your-customer and monitoring controls would handle non-human payment initiators. [single source]

item.title: The SEC settles with Coinbase over its missing Gary Gensler texts
item.description: The United States Securities and Exchange Commission agreed to pay $150,000 and release its remaining records to settle a freedom-of-information lawsuit, brought on behalf of the crypto exchange Coinbase, that sought documents on the agency's crypto investigations and had exposed the regulator's accidental deletion of former chair Gary Gensler's text messages. So what: The resolved dispute removes one overhang from the United States regulator's crypto agenda, but the record-keeping failures it exposed are a caution against assuming a regulator's evidentiary trail from earlier crypto cases is complete when a compliance function weighs how much precedential weight to give those enforcement positions. [single source]

item.title: Goldman Sachs CEO backs Clarity Act despite banking industry's concerns over stablecoin rules
item.description: The chief executive of the investment bank Goldman Sachs publicly endorsed the United States "Clarity Act," a proposed law that would set a federal framework for digital assets, breaking with rival banks such as JPMorgan that object to provisions letting stablecoin issuers effectively pay interest to holders. So what: The split among major United States banks over whether stablecoin issuers should be allowed to pay yield signals that the shape of United States stablecoin regulation remains unsettled, a development worth tracking for any function assessing stablecoin product plans or deposit-competition risk. [single source]

item.title: Strategy, BlackRock form Bitcoin Security Consortium to prepare for quantum computing threat
item.description: Nine major financial and crypto firms, including the asset manager BlackRock, the exchange Coinbase and the Bitcoin-holding company Strategy, formed the "Bitcoin Security Consortium" and pledged $15 million over three years to fund research into protecting Bitcoin against future quantum-computing attacks on its encryption. So what: A coordinated industry push to future-proof Bitcoin's cryptography signals that quantum-computing risk is shifting from theory toward operational planning, useful background for any function assessing the long-term custody and integrity risks of holding or servicing crypto assets. [single source]

item.title: LayerZero, Keeta enable tokenized bank deposits across Ethereum, Solana and Base
item.description: The blockchain-infrastructure firms LayerZero and Keeta announced technology that lets regulated commercial-bank deposits, denominated in the United States dollar and eight other currencies, move across several public blockchains including Ethereum, Solana and Base. So what: Tokenised bank deposits circulating on public blockchains would let regulated money move outside traditional banking rails, raising questions a compliance function will need to answer about transaction monitoring, the transfer of sender and recipient information, and custody controls as such products approach launch. [single source]

item.title: BlackRock, Coinbase, Strategy in a new group pledging $15 million to prepare Bitcoin for quantum threats
item.description: BlackRock, Coinbase, Strategy and six other financial and crypto firms launched a group that will direct $15 million over three years toward research and open-source development aimed at protecting Bitcoin from future quantum-computing threats to its encryption. So what: The pledge shows large institutional holders treating quantum-computing risk as a concrete long-term threat to crypto-asset security, useful context for any function evaluating the durability of custody arrangements over multi-year horizons. [single source]

item.title: Abu Dhabi's Mubadala Capital joins tokenization push as Coinbase takes stake in onchain fund
item.description: Abu Dhabi state investor Mubadala Capital launched a blockchain-based (tokenised) version of one of its private-markets investment strategies for professional investors, and the crypto exchange Coinbase took an undisclosed stake in the roughly $75 million on-chain fund. So what: A large sovereign-linked asset manager moving private-markets funds onto public blockchains adds to the momentum behind tokenised real-world assets, a trend a compliance function should track for its implications on investor due diligence and the monitoring of on-chain fund flows. [single source]

item.title: What FATF’s 7th Crypto Compliance Report Card Means: Enforcement Must Catch Up With Legislation
item.description: An analysis of the Financial Action Task Force (FATF)'s seventh progress report on virtual assets found that although most countries have now passed crypto anti-money-laundering laws, including "travel rule" requirements to share the sender's and recipient's details, enforcement lags far behind, with a majority of those countries having taken no enforcement action and fewer than one in ten fully meeting the international standards. So what: The wide gap between crypto anti-money-laundering laws on paper and their actual enforcement means controls vary sharply by country, so a compliance function should base virtual-asset counterparty and correspondent risk ratings on whether a jurisdiction genuinely supervises and enforces its rules, not merely on whether it has enacted them. [corroborated]

item.title: Circle partners with Kakao, Toss Bank to explore stablecoin payment rails in South Korea
item.description: The stablecoin issuer Circle signed a preliminary agreement with South Korea's Kakao Group and began working with Toss Bank to explore building stablecoin-based payment systems in South Korea, extending Circle's push into the Korean market. So what: Continued expansion of dollar-stablecoin payment rails into major Asian markets increases the likelihood that such instruments feature in cross-border flows, something a compliance function monitoring regional payment channels will want to factor into its stablecoin exposure and screening approach. [single source]

item.title: New Verus-Ethereum bridge attack drains $7.5 million through flaw used in May: Blockaid
item.description: Attackers drained about $7.5 million from the software bridge connecting the Verus and Ethereum blockchains by exploiting the same type of flaw used in an earlier May theft, then laundered much of the proceeds through the crypto mixing service Tornado Cash, according to security firms. So what: The repeat exploitation of an unpatched cross-chain bridge and the routing of proceeds through a mixing service reinforce familiar crypto-laundering typologies, relevant for a compliance function calibrating transaction-monitoring rules for funds that touch bridges and mixers. [single source]

item.title: What WebX Tokyo taught me about Japan’s readiness for on-chain finance
item.description: A compliance-firm analyst's recap of the WebX conference in Tokyo argued that Japan, which has licensed crypto exchanges since 2017 and restricts stablecoin issuance to licensed banks and trust companies, is comparatively well prepared for blockchain-based ("on-chain") finance, while flagging gaps such as the unclear regulatory treatment of prediction markets. So what: Japan's relatively mature crypto regulatory framework offers a reference point for how licensing and stablecoin-issuer restrictions can be structured, useful comparative context for a compliance function tracking how different Asian jurisdictions supervise digital-asset activity. [single source]

item.title: Arbitrum protocol AFX Trade hit by $24 million bridge exploit: Blockaid
item.description: A trading protocol called AFX Trade, built on the Arbitrum blockchain, lost about $24 million after attackers exploited a flaw in a bridge the protocol itself operates (not Arbitrum's core network), moved the funds to Ethereum and converted them to ether, according to security firms. So what: Another multi-million-dollar bridge exploit with rapid conversion of the stolen funds highlights continuing custody and laundering risks around decentralised-finance bridges, relevant for a compliance function assessing exposure to protocols and counterparties that rely on such infrastructure. [single source]

item.title: Senator Lummis: Ethics, other provisions in crypto Clarity Act to be further discussed
item.description: United States Senator Cynthia Lummis said that ethics rules and other contested parts of the "Clarity Act," a proposed digital-asset market-structure law whose merged text was released on 22 July, remain open to negotiation, and that the bill is unlikely to pass before Congress's August recess. So what: The unresolved illicit-finance and ethics provisions in this proposed United States digital-asset law mean its eventual anti-money-laundering requirements are still uncertain, a legislative track a compliance function will want to monitor for future changes to United States crypto oversight. [single source]

item.title: Key Democratic lawmakers say crypto Clarity Act 'falls short' on ethics, other issues
item.description: Seven United States Senate Democrats issued a joint statement saying the proposed "Clarity Act" digital-asset law "falls short" on ethics, consumer protection, illicit-finance safeguards and conflict-of-interest rules, signalling resistance to the Republican-drafted bill. So what: Democratic objections over the bill's anti-money-laundering and integrity provisions could reshape or stall the proposed United States crypto framework, so its illicit-finance requirements should be treated as unsettled by any compliance function planning around future United States digital-asset rules. [single source]
